title: Problem 1 (Dvořák, Norin and Postle)
statement_text: Does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every planar graph $G$ and assignment $L$ of lists of size (a) five in general, (b) four if $G$ is triangle-free, (c) three if $G$ has girth at least five is (weighted) $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Dvořák, Norin, and Postle introduced the notion of $\varepsilon$-flexibility in list coloring and proved that planar graphs with list-size 6 are $\varepsilon$-flexible and planar graphs of girth at least five with list-size 4 are $\varepsilon$-flexible. They raised the question of whether smaller list sizes suffice in these and related settings, noting that the proposed list sizes match the best possible bounds guaranteeing the existence of any coloring from the lists.
paper_contribution: Proves Theorem 2: there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every planar graph of girth at least six with lists of size three is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible, resolving part (c) for girth $\geq 6$ and improving the prior threshold of girth $\geq 12$; part (b) was resolved affirmatively in a companion paper by the same authors.
paper_title: Flexibility of planar graphs of girth at least six
